content:
# Titus Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 222-223 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/TIT_001_page_001.jpg, TIT_001_page_002.jpg

---

**1:1** Paul, a servant of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of the chosen ones of God and the knowledge of the truth that accords with godliness,

**1:2** in hope of eternal life, which God, who cannot lie, promised before the ages of time,

**1:3** but at the proper time manifested his word in the proclamation with which I was entrusted by the command of God our Savior --

**1:4** To Titus, my true child according to the common faith: Grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior.

**1:5** For this reason I left you in Crete, that you might set in order what still remained, and appoint elders in every city, as I myself directed you --

**1:6** if anyone is above reproach, the husband of one wife, having faithful children not under accusation of reckless living or insubordination.

**1:7** For the overseer must be above reproach as God's steward, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given to wine, not violent, not greedy for dishonest gain,

**1:8** but hospitable, a lover of good, self-controlled, upright, holy, disciplined,

**1:9** holding fast the faithful word according to the teaching, so that he may be able both to exhort in sound teaching and to rebuke those who contradict it.

**1:10** For there are many who are insubordinate, empty talkers and deceivers, especially those of the circumcision,

**1:11** who must be silenced, who overturn whole households, teaching things which they ought not, for the sake of dishonest gain.

**1:12** One of them, a prophet of their own, said, "Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons."

**1:13** This testimony is true. For this cause rebuke them sharply, that they may be sound in the faith,

**1:14** not giving heed to Jewish myths and commandments of men who turn away from the truth.

**1:15** To the pure all things are pure, but to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure, but both their mind and their conscience are defiled.

**1:16** They profess to know God, but by their works they deny him, being detestable and disobedient and unfit for any good work.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

No verse in this chapter achieved full three-way identical agreement. Every verse required adjudication among the three translators and is documented in the Disagreement Log below. This is expected: the literal, formal, and dynamic approaches to translation differ by design, producing meaningfully different renderings even when translating the same underlying Greek text faithfully.

**Manuscript verification:** I examined both pages of Codex Sinaiticus (pages 222-223). The text of Titus 1 begins in column 2 of page 222 and continues through columns 3-4, with the chapter concluding at the top of page 223. The manuscript is legible throughout with no significant damage or lacunae. I confirm: (1) ΔΟΥΛΟΣ ΘΕΟΥ in v.1; (2) no ΕΛΕΟΣ ("mercy") in v.4 -- the reading is ΧΑΡΙΣ ΚΑΙ ΕΙΡΗΝΗ only; (3) the Epimenides quotation in v.12 is intact. All three translators correctly read the manuscript.

---

## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1:1
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Paul, a slave of God, and an apostle of Jesus Christ, according-to [the] faith of [the] chosen-ones of God and [the] full-knowledge of [the] truth, the [truth] according-to godliness" |
| LOGOS | "Paul, a servant of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of the elect of God and the knowledge of the truth that accords with godliness" |
| PNEUMA | "From Paul, a servant of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ -- sent to strengthen the faith of God's chosen people and to deepen their knowledge of the truth that leads to a godly life" |

**Divergences:** (1) EMET renders ΔΟΥΛΟΣ as "slave"; LOGOS and PNEUMA render it "servant." SOPHIA selects "servant" -- while "slave" is etymologically precise, "servant" is the standard formal equivalent that preserves the relational sense without anachronistic connotations. (2) EMET uses "full-knowledge" for ΕΠΙΓΝΩΣΙΝ; LOGOS uses "knowledge"; PNEUMA paraphrases as "deepen their knowledge." SOPHIA selects "knowledge" -- the prefix ΕΠΙ- adds intensity but "full-knowledge" is awkward English. (3) EMET uses "chosen-ones"; LOGOS uses "elect"; PNEUMA uses "God's chosen people." SOPHIA selects "chosen ones" -- more readable than "elect" while retaining the substantive force. (4) PNEUMA restructures the entire verse into purposive clauses ("sent to strengthen... to deepen"), departing from the Greek syntax. SOPHIA retains the prepositional structure "according to." (5) PNEUMA adds "From" before Paul; the Greek has no preposition here.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as the base, with "chosen ones" from EMET for ΕΚΛΕΚΤΩΝ.

---

### Verse 1:2
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "upon hope of eternal life, which the unlying God promised before times eternal" |
| LOGOS | "in hope of eternal life, which God, who does not lie, promised before the ages of time" |
| PNEUMA | "resting on the hope of eternal life that God, who cannot lie, promised before time itself began" |

**Divergences:** (1) Preposition: EMET "upon"; LOGOS "in"; PNEUMA "resting on." Greek ΕΠ' (epi) with dative supports "in" or "upon." SOPHIA selects "in" as the most natural formal rendering. (2) ΑΨΕΥΔΗΣ: EMET "unlying" (neologism preserving alpha-privative); LOGOS "who does not lie" (relative clause); PNEUMA "who cannot lie" (stronger modal claim). SOPHIA selects "who cannot lie" -- it captures the essential quality (inability to lie, not merely absence of lying) while being clear English. (3) ΠΡΟ ΧΡΟΝΩΝ ΑΙΩΝΙΩΝ: EMET "before times eternal"; LOGOS "before the ages of time"; PNEUMA "before time itself began." SOPHIA selects "before the ages of time" as the closest formal rendering. (4) PNEUMA adds "resting on" and restructures as a participial continuation from v.1.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as the base, adopting PNEUMA's "who cannot lie" for ΑΨΕΥΔΗΣ.

---

### Verse 1:3
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "but manifested in [its] own seasons his word, in [the] proclamation which I was entrusted [with] according-to [the] command of our savior God" |
| LOGOS | "but at the proper time manifested his word in the proclamation with which I was entrusted by the command of God our Savior" |
| PNEUMA | "and which he revealed at just the right moment through the proclamation entrusted to me by the command of God our Savior" |

**Divergences:** (1) ΚΑΙΡΟΙΣ ΙΔΙΟΙΣ: EMET "in its own seasons"; LOGOS "at the proper time"; PNEUMA "at just the right moment." SOPHIA selects "at the proper time." (2) ΕΦΑΝΕΡΩΣΕΝ: EMET/LOGOS "manifested"; PNEUMA "revealed." SOPHIA selects "manifested" as the more precise equivalent. (3) Word order for "God our Savior": EMET puts "our savior God"; LOGOS/PNEUMA put "God our Savior." The Greek reads ΤΟΥ ΣΩΤΗΡΟΣ ΗΜΩΝ ΘΕΟΥ. SOPHIA follows the LOGOS/PNEUMA word order as more natural English. (4) PNEUMA uses "through" instead of "in" for ΕΝ ΚΗΡΥΓΜΑΤΙ.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:4
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to Titus, [my] true child according-to [the] common faith: grace and peace from God [the] Father and Christ Jesus our savior." |
| LOGOS | "To Titus, my true child according to a common faith: Grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior." |
| PNEUMA | "To Titus, my true son in the faith we share: May grace and peace be yours from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΤΕΚΝΟΝ: EMET/LOGOS "child"; PNEUMA "son." SOPHIA selects "child" -- ΤΕΚΝΟΝ is gender-neutral in Greek. (2) ΚΟΙΝΗΝ ΠΙΣΤΙΝ: EMET "the common faith"; LOGOS "a common faith"; PNEUMA "the faith we share." SOPHIA selects "the common faith" with the definite article, reflecting the shared doctrinal faith. (3) PNEUMA adds "May... be yours" (optative expansion); EMET/LOGOS simply state "grace and peace." SOPHIA follows the simpler declaration. (4) LOGOS uses "a" (indefinite); EMET uses "the" (definite). The Greek has no article, but the context points to a specific shared faith.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as the base, with the definite article "the common faith."

---

### Verse 1:5
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For this cause I left-behind you in Crete, that the [things] lacking you might set-in-order, and might appoint in every city elders, as I myself commanded you" |
| LOGOS | "For this reason I left you in Crete, that you might set in order the things that remain and appoint elders in every city, as I directed you" |
| PNEUMA | "I left you behind in Crete for this reason: to put in order what still needed to be set right, and to appoint elders in every town, following the guidelines I gave you." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΤΟΥΤΟΥ ΧΑΡΙΝ: EMET "for this cause"; LOGOS "for this reason"; PNEUMA "for this reason" (repositioned). All acceptable; SOPHIA selects "for this reason." (2) ΤΑ ΛΕΙΠΟΝΤΑ: EMET "the things lacking"; LOGOS "the things that remain"; PNEUMA "what still needed to be set right." SOPHIA selects "what still remained" -- combining clarity with fidelity. (3) ΠΟΛΙΝ: EMET/LOGOS "city"; PNEUMA "town." SOPHIA selects "city." (4) EMET "commanded"; LOGOS "directed"; PNEUMA "following the guidelines I gave you." SOPHIA selects "directed." (5) EMET adds "myself" (reflecting the Greek emphatic ΕΓΩ); SOPHIA retains "I myself" to preserve this emphasis.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as the base, with "I myself" from EMET and "what still remained" as a synthesis.

---

### Verse 1:6
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "if anyone is without-accusation, of-one-wife [a] husband, having faithful children, not under accusation of reckless-living or unruly." |
| LOGOS | "if anyone is above reproach, the husband of one wife, having faithful children not under accusation of profligacy or insubordination." |
| PNEUMA | "An elder must be above reproach -- faithful to his wife, with children who are believers and not open to charges of wild living or defiance." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΑΝΕΓΚΛΗΤΟΣ: EMET "without-accusation"; LOGOS/PNEUMA "above reproach." SOPHIA selects "above reproach" -- EMET's hyphenated form is technically accurate but stilted. (2) ΜΙΑΣ ΓΥΝΑΙΚΟΣ ΑΝΗΡ: EMET "of-one-wife a husband"; LOGOS "the husband of one wife"; PNEUMA "faithful to his wife" (interpretive paraphrase). SOPHIA selects "the husband of one wife" -- PNEUMA's rendering, while capturing the spirit, imports an interpretation not explicit in the text. (3) ΠΙΣΤΑ ΤΕΚΝΑ: EMET/LOGOS "faithful children"; PNEUMA "children who are believers." SOPHIA selects "faithful" -- the ambiguity is in the Greek and should be preserved. (4) ΑΣΩΤΙΑΣ: EMET "reckless-living"; LOGOS "profligacy"; PNEUMA "wild living." SOPHIA selects "reckless living" -- clearer than "profligacy" and more precise than "wild living." (5) ΑΝΥΠΟΤΑΚΤΑ: EMET "unruly"; LOGOS "insubordination"; PNEUMA "defiance." SOPHIA selects "insubordination" as the closest formal match.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS as the base, with "reckless living" as a synthesis of EMET and PNEUMA.

---

### Verse 1:7
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For it-is-necessary [for] the overseer to be without-accusation, as God's house-steward, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given-to-wine, not a striker, not greedy-for-shameful-gain" |
| LOGOS | "For the overseer must be above reproach as God's steward, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given to wine, not violent, not greedy for dishonest gain" |
| PNEUMA | "Since an overseer manages God's household, he must be above reproach: not arrogant, not hot-tempered, not a heavy drinker, not violent, and not greedy for money." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΟΝ: EMET "house-steward"; LOGOS "steward"; PNEUMA "manages God's household" (verbal paraphrase). SOPHIA selects "steward." (2) ΑΝΕΓΚΛΗΤΟΣ: same divergence as v.6. (3) ΑΥΘΑΔΗ: EMET/LOGOS "self-willed"; PNEUMA "arrogant." SOPHIA selects "self-willed" -- it is closer to the Greek etymology (self-pleasing). (4) ΟΡΓΙΛΟΝ: EMET/LOGOS "quick-tempered"; PNEUMA "hot-tempered." Both acceptable; SOPHIA selects "quick-tempered." (5) ΠΑΡΟΙΝΟΝ: EMET "given-to-wine"; LOGOS "given to wine"; PNEUMA "a heavy drinker." SOPHIA selects "given to wine." (6) ΠΛΗΚΤΗΝ: EMET "a striker"; LOGOS/PNEUMA "violent." SOPHIA selects "violent" -- "striker" is archaic. (7) ΑΙΣΧΡΟΚΕΡΔΗ: EMET "greedy-for-shameful-gain"; LOGOS "greedy for dishonest gain"; PNEUMA "greedy for money." SOPHIA selects "greedy for dishonest gain."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:8
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "but hospitable, a lover-of-good, sound-minded, righteous, holy, self-controlled" |
| LOGOS | "but hospitable, a lover of good, self-controlled, upright, holy, disciplined" |
| PNEUMA | "Instead, he must be hospitable, a lover of what is good, self-controlled, upright, devout, and disciplined." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΣΩΦΡΟΝΑ: EMET "sound-minded"; LOGOS "self-controlled"; PNEUMA "self-controlled." SOPHIA selects "self-controlled." (2) ΔΙΚΑΙΟΝ: EMET "righteous"; LOGOS/PNEUMA "upright." SOPHIA selects "upright" -- in the context of character qualifications, "upright" is the more natural English. (3) ΟΣΙΟΝ: EMET "holy"; LOGOS "holy"; PNEUMA "devout." SOPHIA selects "holy" -- ΟΣΙΟΣ connotes consecrated devotion, and "holy" preserves the distinct term from ΑΓΙΟΣ. (4) ΕΓΚΡΑΤΗ: EMET "self-controlled"; LOGOS "disciplined"; PNEUMA "disciplined." SOPHIA selects "disciplined" -- since ΣΩΦΡΟΝΑ is already rendered "self-controlled," using "disciplined" for ΕΓΚΡΑΤΗ avoids repetition. (5) Word order: EMET follows the Greek order (sound-minded, righteous, holy, self-controlled); LOGOS/PNEUMA rearrange slightly. SOPHIA follows the LOGOS order. (6) PNEUMA adds "Instead" and "he must be" for contextual flow.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:9
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "holding-fast the faithful word according-to the teaching, that he might be able both to exhort in the sound teaching and to convict those contradicting." |
| LOGOS | "holding fast the faithful word according to the teaching, so that he may be able both to exhort in sound doctrine and to rebuke those who contradict it." |
| PNEUMA | "He must hold firmly to the trustworthy message as it has been taught, so that he is able both to encourage others with sound teaching and to expose those who contradict it." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΤΟΥ ΠΙΣΤΟΥ ΛΟΓΟΥ: EMET/LOGOS "the faithful word"; PNEUMA "the trustworthy message." SOPHIA selects "the faithful word." (2) ΚΑΤΑ ΤΗΝ ΔΙΔΑΧΗΝ: EMET/LOGOS "according to the teaching"; PNEUMA "as it has been taught." SOPHIA selects "according to the teaching." (3) ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΕΙΝ: EMET/LOGOS "exhort"; PNEUMA "encourage." SOPHIA selects "exhort" -- it carries the stronger sense of ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΕΩ in a doctrinal context. (4) ΤΗ ΔΙΔΑΣΚΑΛΙΑ ΤΗ ΥΓΙΑΙΝΟΥΣΗ: EMET "the sound teaching"; LOGOS "sound doctrine"; PNEUMA "sound teaching." SOPHIA selects "sound teaching." (5) ΕΛΕΓΧΕΙΝ: EMET "convict"; LOGOS "rebuke"; PNEUMA "expose." SOPHIA selects "rebuke" -- it captures the confrontational sense of ΕΛΕΓΧΩ without the legal overtone of "convict." (6) ΤΟΥΣ ΑΝΤΙΛΕΓΟΝΤΑΣ: EMET "those contradicting"; LOGOS/PNEUMA "those who contradict it." SOPHIA adds "it" for clarity.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS, with "sound teaching" instead of "sound doctrine."

---

### Verse 1:10
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For there are many both unruly and vain-talkers and mind-deceivers, especially those of the circumcision" |
| LOGOS | "For there are many who are insubordinate, empty talkers and deceivers, especially those of the circumcision" |
| PNEUMA | "For there are many rebellious people out there -- idle talkers and deceivers -- especially those from the circumcision party." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΑΝΥΠΟΤΑΚΤΟΙ: EMET "unruly"; LOGOS "insubordinate"; PNEUMA "rebellious." SOPHIA selects "insubordinate" for consistency with v.6. (2) ΜΑΤΑΙΟΛΟΓΟΙ: EMET "vain-talkers"; LOGOS "empty talkers"; PNEUMA "idle talkers." SOPHIA selects "empty talkers." (3) ΦΡΕΝΑΠΑΤΑΙ: EMET "mind-deceivers"; LOGOS/PNEUMA "deceivers." SOPHIA selects "deceivers" -- the compound force is captured in context. (4) PNEUMA adds "people out there" and "party" (interpretive additions). (5) ΟΙ ΕΚ ΤΗΣ ΠΕΡΙΤΟΜΗΣ: EMET/LOGOS "those of the circumcision"; PNEUMA "those from the circumcision party." SOPHIA selects "those of the circumcision."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:11
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "whom it-is-necessary to silence, who overturn whole houses, teaching things which [they] ought not, for-the-sake-of shameful gain." |
| LOGOS | "who must be silenced, who overturn whole households, teaching things which they ought not, for the sake of dishonest gain." |
| PNEUMA | "They must be silenced, because they are ruining entire households by teaching things they have no business teaching, and they do it for shameful profit." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΟΙΚΟΥΣ: EMET "houses"; LOGOS "households"; PNEUMA "households." SOPHIA selects "households." (2) ΑΙΣΧΡΟΥ ΚΕΡΔΟΥΣ: EMET "shameful gain"; LOGOS "dishonest gain"; PNEUMA "shameful profit." SOPHIA selects "dishonest gain" for consistency with v.7. (3) PNEUMA substantially restructures the sentence and adds interpretive language ("have no business teaching," "they do it for"). (4) PNEUMA uses "ruining" for ΑΝΑΤΡΕΠΟΥΣΙΝ; EMET/LOGOS use "overturn." SOPHIA selects "overturn" as more literal.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:12
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Said a certain-one of them, their own prophet: 'Cretans [are] always liars, evil beasts, idle bellies.'" |
| LOGOS | "One of them, a prophet of their own, said, 'Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, idle gluttons.'" |
| PNEUMA | "One of their own prophets said it: 'Cretans are always liars, vicious beasts, and lazy gluttons.'" |

**Divergences:** (1) Sentence structure: EMET front-loads "Said" (Greek word order); LOGOS/PNEUMA use standard English SVO order. SOPHIA follows standard order. (2) ΚΑΚΑ ΘΗΡΙΑ: EMET/LOGOS "evil beasts"; PNEUMA "vicious beasts." SOPHIA selects "evil beasts" -- ΚΑΚΑ is more broadly "evil/bad" than "vicious." (3) ΓΑΣΤΕΡΕΣ ΑΡΓΑΙ: EMET "idle bellies" (strictly literal); LOGOS "idle gluttons"; PNEUMA "lazy gluttons." SOPHIA selects "lazy gluttons" -- "idle bellies" is too opaque for English readers, and "lazy" better captures ΑΡΓΑΙ (from A-ΕΡΓΟΣ, "without work") than "idle" in modern usage. (4) PNEUMA adds "and" before the third item; EMET/LOGOS maintain asyndeton. SOPHIA omits the conjunction to preserve the Greek rhetorical effect.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Synthesize: "One of them, a prophet of their own, said, 'Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons.'"

---

### Verse 1:13
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "This testimony is true. For which cause rebuke them sharply, that they might be sound in the faith" |
| LOGOS | "This testimony is true. For this cause rebuke them sharply, that they may be sound in the faith" |
| PNEUMA | "And that testimony is accurate! So confront them sharply, so that they become healthy in the faith" |

**Divergences:** (1) ΑΛΗΘΗΣ: EMET/LOGOS "true"; PNEUMA "accurate." SOPHIA selects "true." (2) ΕΛΕΓΧΕ: EMET/LOGOS "rebuke"; PNEUMA "confront." SOPHIA selects "rebuke." (3) ΥΓΙΑΙΝΩΣΙΝ: EMET/LOGOS "be sound"; PNEUMA "become healthy." SOPHIA selects "be sound" -- the health metaphor is present in "sound" (from the same semantic domain) and is the established rendering in translation tradition. (4) PNEUMA adds "And" and "!" for stylistic effect. (5) ΔΙ' ΗΝ ΑΙΤΙΑΝ: EMET "for which cause"; LOGOS "for this cause"; PNEUMA "So." SOPHIA selects "for this cause."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:14
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "not giving-heed to Jewish myths and commandments of men turning-themselves-away-from the truth." |
| LOGOS | "not giving heed to Jewish myths and commandments of men who turn away from the truth." |
| PNEUMA | "and stop devoting themselves to Jewish myths and to the commands of people who turn their backs on the truth." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΠΡΟΣΕΧΟΝΤΕΣ: EMET/LOGOS "giving heed"; PNEUMA "devoting themselves." SOPHIA selects "giving heed." (2) ΕΝΤΟΛΑΙΣ: EMET/LOGOS "commandments"; PNEUMA "commands." SOPHIA selects "commandments." (3) ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ: EMET/LOGOS "men"; PNEUMA "people." SOPHIA selects "men" -- the Greek ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ can be generic, but "men" is the traditional rendering in this formulaic phrase. (4) ΑΠΟΣΤΡΕΦΟΜΕΝΩΝ: EMET "turning-themselves-away-from"; LOGOS "who turn away from"; PNEUMA "who turn their backs on." SOPHIA selects "who turn away from." (5) PNEUMA restructures with "and stop" (making it an independent clause).

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:15
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "All [things are] clean to the clean; but to the ones having-been-defiled and unbelieving, nothing [is] clean, but both their mind and [their] conscience have-been-defiled." |
| LOGOS | "To the pure all things are pure, but to the defiled and unbelieving nothing is pure, but both their mind and their conscience are defiled." |
| PNEUMA | "To the pure, everything is pure. But to those who are corrupt and faithless, nothing is pure -- even their minds and consciences are polluted." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΚΑΘΑΡΑ/ΚΑΘΑΡΟΙΣ: EMET "clean/clean"; LOGOS "pure/pure"; PNEUMA "pure/pure." SOPHIA selects "pure" -- it better captures the ritual and moral sense of ΚΑΘΑΡΟΣ. (2) Word order: EMET follows Greek order ("All things clean to the clean"); LOGOS/PNEUMA front "To the pure." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. (3) ΜΕΜΙΑΜΜΕΝΟΙΣ: EMET "having-been-defiled" (preserving perfect tense); LOGOS "defiled"; PNEUMA "corrupt." SOPHIA selects "defiled." (4) ΑΠΙΣΤΟΙΣ: EMET "unbelieving"; LOGOS "unbelieving"; PNEUMA "faithless." SOPHIA selects "unbelieving." (5) ΜΕΜΙΑΝΤΑΙ: EMET "have-been-defiled" (perfect); LOGOS "are defiled"; PNEUMA "are polluted." SOPHIA selects "are defiled" -- the perfect tense is captured by the stative "are defiled" in English. (6) PNEUMA uses "everything/nothing" instead of "all things/nothing." (7) PNEUMA uses "even" instead of "but both."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

### Verse 1:16
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "[They] profess to know God, but by [their] works they deny [him], being abominable and disobedient and for every good work worthless." |
| LOGOS | "They profess to know God, but by their works they deny him, being detestable and disobedient and unfit for any good work." |
| PNEUMA | "They claim to know God, but their actions tell a different story. They are detestable, disobedient, and worthless for doing anything good." |

**Divergences:** (1) ΟΜΟΛΟΓΟΥΣΙΝ: EMET/LOGOS "profess"; PNEUMA "claim." SOPHIA selects "profess" -- it carries the public, confessional weight of ΟΜΟΛΟΓΕΩ. (2) ΕΡΓΟΙΣ ΑΡΝΟΥΝΤΑΙ: EMET/LOGOS "by their works they deny him"; PNEUMA "their actions tell a different story" (free paraphrase). SOPHIA retains the direct rendering "by their works they deny him." (3) ΒΔΕΛΥΚΤΟΙ: EMET "abominable"; LOGOS/PNEUMA "detestable." SOPHIA selects "detestable" -- while "abominable" has strong OT resonance, "detestable" is equally strong and more immediately accessible. (4) ΑΔΟΚΙΜΟΙ: EMET "worthless"; LOGOS "unfit"; PNEUMA "worthless." SOPHIA selects "unfit" -- ΑΔΟΚΙΜΟΣ means "failing the test, disqualified," which "unfit" captures better than "worthless." (5) EMET restructures the final clause ("for every good work worthless"); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "unfit/worthless for any good work." SOPHIA follows LOGOS word order.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Follow LOGOS.

---

*So it is rendered, by the council of three, and the judgment of SOPHIA.*
